speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three, Santa Pelham, Cape Town. OK. Santa, I'd like you to continue the story with Renia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Renia kept on coming to the house, and then she befriended me personally. And she asked me if I have some beau, some gentleman. I said no. I do go out. I have many young people here, refugees as well. And-- it's not a monastic life that I'm leading. But then-- there-- there's really nobody for me. All we want to do now is that ORT-- we want to go to South America because we can't get into North America. It was gesperrt. It was closed for Jews, especially, and Canada, definitely. And so we thought, well, we go to-- to South America where they speak Spanish. And then we'll work our way up to-- to USA, because we wanted to go to the USA to-- I don't know why, but that was in our minds. So the ORT again started working, that we should be able to go to South America, to Montevideo, which is Uruguay, or to Para-- what-- whatever you call these places-- Chile, or-- these Spanish-- to Spanish-speaking places. Argentina was out of bounds. I don't know why. But Uruguay, Paraguay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Chile?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chile and others. These-- in the middle. They were not developed. There, they would allow Jews to come. And so the ORT was working for that. Of course, you needed passage on a boat. They were willing to pay. The-- the money was there for the-- for the refugees. They were wonderful. But they said if you would go singly or in pairs, it would be easier to get on because a family of five is not-- not easy. We refused. We were silly. We said, no. We want to go as a family. We were already separated twice. No, thank you. We want to go as a family. And they worked for it. But it was too late when-- you know, it just wasn't possible. So what happened then was-- [PAUSES] as I said, Renia was working on me, pulling out of me what kind of a girl is this. Not-- I didn't know why she was doing it. I thought it was just friendly talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she had a purpose. Now, she-- one day she came, and she said, look, my husband had to go to Leon for business. Will you come and ke-- have supper with me tonight? Keep me company? So I said, with pleasure. I went up there. We sat down. And as we were talking, she said, you know, I've got a young man for you. I said, yes? Where are you hiding him, under the table? And she laughed, and she says, no, he's far away. He is in Rhodesia, southern Rhodesia. I've never heard of southern Rhodesia. I said, but where is this southern Rhodesia? I mean, I wasn't even interested in a young man. I wanted to know this place. What is this place, southern Rhodesia? She says, oh, it's somewhere where the Congo is. Now, what comes into your mind, Congo? Palms, lions, blacks running with the-- the limp cloths-- what do you call it? Loincloths?

speaker: interviewee
text: Loincloths, yes. And so on. And the women bouncing their-- their-- their long-- longer tits. All this flashed through my mind. I said, but that's in Africa! She says, yes, of course it's in Africa.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, so, what have-- what did you have in mind? She says, well, there is a young man, she said, who is a prince. He is a wonderful guy. We love him. He is the brother of my brother-in-law. Her sister was married to Jack's brother. So I said, but-- but listen, this is-- this is something that is impossible. I'm not really interested in Rhodesia. We want to go to-- to South America. We want to go in a-- they're working for us. And we knew-- we were sure we were going together because ORT was working for it, and they were.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it couldn't be done. So anyhow, we didn't speak any more about this, and I left it. We-- we left it. And I went home and I said, Renia is a little bit not right in her head. Somebody in Rhodesia. What nonsense. Anyhow, she had also asked me what kind of boys I like, what kind-- what do I desire in a man. I said, he has got to be tall and dark and handsome-- a prince. [LAUGHS] And she said, he's everything you-- he's-- he is a prince, like you want, but he-- all the other things, he's not. He's short. He's good looking, but he hasn't got much hair. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So there, it was gone. I said, definitely not. Forget about it. This is nonsense. Anyhow, I then talk to my mother. I was really close with my mother. I talked to her about it and she laughed. She laughed.

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't take a few days later when the police came. I wasn't home at that time. The pol-- I already had my-- my certificate for my-- my manicurist, if you don't mind. I had a certificate, and I had already clientele, mostly Jewish people, French people who could afford. And I brought these few francs home towards living.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the police came to est-- arrest my father and my brother, Leon, who had-- who was working at his Spanish visa, they took him in. And they said, but why? What for? Because you are working illegally. So they-- they took them into prison.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came home, my mother was in tears. Look what happened! So I went to the prefecture. And I came up there and you go to a-- an anteroom, and there are police sitting and they are joking with their helmets on. You know, the-- the-- the-- the way-- the way the Fren-- the French police look.

speaker: interviewee
text: And young people, all young policemen. And they're joking. I said, I would like to see my father and my brother. Is it possible? We'll find out. And it took a little time. You have to wait. It was lunch time. And one of them laughed like anything. He said, would you like some French coffee? Now, I didn't know at that time what meant French coffee, but I had a good inkling. I ran out. I'm sure nothing will happen for me seeing my father because it was already hours that I was there, and they were out for something.

speaker: interviewee
text: They wanted sex to see my father. Well, I wasn't prepared to give sex. And I ran out, crying, crying, like anything. And then they have this big portal, iron portal. And I stood there sobbing, sobbing. I hadn't seen my parents-- my father and my brother. I can't help them. And this insult on me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And an elderly man of about 50 came up to me. He says, mademoiselle, why are you crying-- in French, of course-- why are you crying? And I pour out my heart, my bitter heart, what happened. Oh, he said, you poor girl. You know, I will help you. I-- I've got a-- an apartamente underground, [FRENCH], something like this, whatever it is. And I will help you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I looked at this man. I start running again. Well, a few days later, my par-- my father and brother were released with the proviso not to work. You are not to work. Of course, they were right. Because all these thousands and thousands, or millions of refugees that poured into France, and in Paris especially, they took away the work from the-- from the Frenchman. And it was already pre-war, and the rumors of war were going strong in 1938. It was end-- end of 1938. And you know-- and they had to look after their own. But I didn't understand that. For me, my father and-- we-- we couldn't work. We couldn't do a thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where are we going to live from? From my few francs and from my mother's jar? We got peanuts. We got nothing. And my brother was-- thank God my brother was-- the youngest one-- was at least satiated. He ate. He ate whatever was left over. He ate. He was all right. He was very tall and he became strong. Now, that's when my mother spoke to me. And she said to me, you know, Renia? Renia proposed something to you. You know, she-- what she meant, she didn't want to be saved. She meant me, I should be saved. Her precious daughter should be saved.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, you know, there is something in it, what she says. If she says he's so wonderful, as she says. But now actually what she had done, she had gone before already. I think it must have been during the time that my father and brother were still imprisoned. She went to Renia and said, tell me about this young man. And Renia says, look your-- your-- your daughter will be a-- a queen with him. He is a wonderful person. He is a worker. He is-- I know him very well because he is the one-- he is the one who helped all his brothers to come out of Poland, and then eventually Palestine. He made-- he established them. And he is just marvelous, and your daughter will be good, will be fine with him. My mother thought, it's-- it's a proposition. And then she said, look, sit down. Write a few words. Take a photo. Put it in an envelope. Give it to Renia, and she will try it. And I said, OK. Now she says, it's only adventure. So I said, OK, I will do it. I took a photo, which I have here, and I wrote, 'Dear Mr. Pelham--' He was Pelham by then. He was [INAUDIBLE] before, but-- 'Dear Mr. Pelham, Madame Puter--' In French, by the way-- 'Madame Puterflam has spoken to me about you. She feels that we should know each other. If you are interested, write to me.' Boom, boom. That was all I did. And back came a letter, a beautiful letter, saying he's really interested. He would like to-- to have a-- a little bit more correspondence. But, as you know, through letters there can't be love. There can't be anything. So he is proposing that we should write a letter or two, and then maybe something will come out of it. But his letter was so sensible. Now, he says, if I propose to you, I can offer you only three things. That is a dress, a pair of shoes, and a cinema ticket. And I had to laugh, the honesty of this man! Somebody else would promise the world. Nothing! I had to laugh. And I thought to myself, you know, this sounds good. Maybe I can help my parents. Maybe if I do this, and even if I won't love him, maybe I can get them out in-- and we didn't think the war would start there and then, you see. It was '38. There were rumors, but we thought well, it will take a long time, Hitler won't-- won't attack. There might come something. Hitler is very big in his country, but not really a war. People were-- France wasn't prepared. England wasn't prepared. Nobody was prepared.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I thought I'd get them out this way. And I will persuade him. I'll be very nice to this gentleman, this Mr. Pelham. And I will persuade him to bring my family out. That was what I thought to come. And so it was-- was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we wrote eight letters. During that time, my father found some work as a Holz hacker. How do you call to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wood cutter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wood cutter. Chaim, of course, was in the restaurant. Leon went-- tell you what Leon did. Leon went to work for Puterflam. Puterflam had a huge business of schmatters. Schmatters is fall outs from the factories. And he had to sort out the-- the-- the-- the-- like in the Bible, the goat from the-- with Jacob, the sheep from the goat, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: So the wool here and the linen here and so on. And one day, somebody came running. The police is here! The police is here! So what did he do? He sprang in one of those huge bails and covered himself. He nearly suffocated.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what happens to refugees-- Nazis-- Nazi persecution. He nearly suffocated in there until somebody pulled him out and-- and had to revive him. Because he was afraid to come up. The police had gone, had found nothing. Police were looking for worker-- workers that took away the work from the-- from the French people. I couldn't blame them, but still.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that is what Leon did. When I left, it was Yom Kippur. It was absolute Yom Kippur. I had eight letters from my husband after the-- those two previous ones. In each one, I had said yes, right, send the papers. In each letter was a pound. A pound, English pound, was a lot of money.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these eight pounds, my father and mother went with me to buy a trousseau. And I had a full trousseau for his money. I couldn't take the bit of money that we had-- hardly any-- and the pesetas were still worthless. And so it was that with this trousseau, eventually came the day for me to leave. That evening, we sat there on the floor as if somebody had died. And we cried the whole evening. Now, my parents were very religious.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, they always kept kosher. We didn't keep kosher in France. We couldn't. We didn't have the kilim and we didn't have the means. So when-- why did I mention kosher? You were leaving--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were on the point of leaving.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next day-- oh yes. It's because my father was very, very religious. He didn't want me to wear the short dresses that were in-- very modern at that time, knees up. Oh, no. I wasn't allowed. He was a bit of a religious person, you see. He was from the Chelm, from Poland, where they-- where they were very observant. And my mother had kept a very observant kosher home in Germany. And so it was that in-- when we came on to the station, I said goodbye to everybody. And Renia was there with a bouquet of flowers. What I could do with the flowers, I don't know. But she was in heaven that this was coming off. She thought, this is made in heaven. And-- and I-- I already went up on the steps to go when my father called me. And I turned around, and there was my father standing. He says, promise me here that you will light candles on the Friday night. Because I had become a little bit of a disbeliever.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where is this God? Where is this God that's doing this to us? Why? You have been wonderful people all along. And I've been crying to them. My mother was with me. She understood me. But my father was rigid. No, no. You dare not talk like this. He was a God man. And I had started disbelieving. I said, there is no God to allow this. We? Chosen? For what? Chosen to suffer? We are chosen to set examples, and here we suffer for these examples? Look at the examples we are giving, and look what is coming out of this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father was very upset. That is why, when I was on that last step, turn and he said, look at me. Promise me you will light the candles on Friday night. I say, I promise. And I had, then every-- every Friday night. And my children do it. And that is a good thing, because this is tradition, and it is the continuation of Judaism. And may-- long may it last, forever and ever, because this is what is the example to the world, really. The world maybe doesn't know about it, but this is what keeps it alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father was satisfied. At least I had promised him that I would light the candles. My mother was devastated. And the train went on the four hours journey to Besancon. Oh yes, I was to wait-- I was to stop my journey to Marseilles, where I was catching the boat. I was to stop at Besancon, where this brother-in-law of Renia and-- and his wife were living. This-- the wife was the sister of Renia, you see? And another brother who was a rabbi, but had given it up for something else, was also living there. He was the eldest brother, Sim, the rabbi. And Ignatz was older than-- the-- the brother-in-law of Renia. He was also older than my husband. Anyhow, I arrived there with eyes like that. My sister-in-law comes up to me and she says, what are you crying for? Could I answer her? Could she understand the pain of the suffering of leaving-- of Germany and going through the war and living in-- in-- in Spain incognito. Not as Jews, but as Marranos, keeping our Judaism in the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Spain-- and they knew we were alemanes. They knew we were refugiados alemanes-- German refugees. They did not know we were Jews because the Jews were hated still. It was inculcated in the church that the Jews killed Christ, and the Jews gave away Jesus, and therefore, the Jews are not worth-- they deserve all the pain because of Jesus 2,000 years ago. Anyhow, what happened then was that--

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES] You were on the point of leaving--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I arrived now. And of course, I was heart sore leaving my dear family, having a feeling I wouldn't see them anymore, especially my parents. I thought, will I-- will I ever see them? With a feeling in-- in my gut, I will never see these people again. And then I spent two days with this family. They were wonderful to me, this future sister-in-law. And I had to sing to them because I have a beautiful voice. And they say that Jacob, Jack-- Jack also has a beautiful voice. And the two of us have beautiful voices, and we gave it over to Aviva, who is now the film-- the-- the-- the opera singer here. And so it was. And I left two days after with lots of goodwill, and on the boat now to-- up to-- going to [PAUSES] Rhodesia. And I left my parents. I left my parents there. On the boat, the first day, I just cried the whole day through-- whole day through. And arriving in port side, all of a sudden, lots of Jews arrived from Palestine. Lots of Jews. Some visiting from Johannesburg and from Durban. There were lots of Jews who had visited Palestine. It was Palestine at that time. And lots of others who had come from Latvia and had family in northern Rhodesia. They were also there. But many-- another one had-- also was going to northern Rhodesia. Nobody was going to southern Rhodesia, on-- only-- only this-- this-- this beautiful bride to be. And they came on. And immediately, there was a bond. We immediately stuck together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were talking about Zionism. Now, although Zionism is not the same here, I assure you it is. Because-- because of this Zionism, we have Israel today. We would not have Israel if it weren't for Zionism developing out of the Holocaust and establishing it. It's the Holocaust that brought about-- with Zionism working to be brought about the establishment of Israel, our country, for over what? 4,000 years? You see? So anyhow, here they're talking about Zionism. My parents were very religious. They don't-- didn't talk about Zionism. I had heard Zionism here, Zionism here. I had-- while I was in-- in Cologne, when my parents had gone already, there was a young man, my age, who-- we were not friends. We were just children.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had taken me into the Hashomer Hatzair youth club, which is a communist youth club. And once we went on a picnic and they were all-- we had to-- because it is a communist pl-- place, we had to put all our sandwiches together. My aunt had made me-- oh, I made the sandwiches. They were ordinary sandwiches with cheese or whatever. They were lying there. And somebody had brought sandwiches with ham. And as we were allowed to now to go and take sandwiches, they dived for the ham. And I was horrified. Me, the frumer one. I am not going to eat ham. And how dare they! It's forbidden. I was 15 at that time, 16-- 15 years. Anyhow, I looked for my sandwiches, but they were gone also. And I was afraid to eat the others, so I went hungry. That is Zionism for you, youth Zionism. And now we are in port side, and these Jews come up there. And we are very close. Zionism. Zionism. My eyes are spinning with it. And they explained to me that general Zion-- Zionism is the best. And there is, of course, Revisionism. Oh, you must not belong to the Revisionists. This is terrible. They want war. They didn't want war. They wanted the country. And without fighting, you couldn't get that country, unfortunately. I wish we could have got it without the fighting, but they wouldn't give it up. And so I learned about Zionism. And on the boat ride through until Beira, where I was getting off, we stuck together. We made friends, and it was wonderful. Coming off in Beira, I was supposed-- oh, yes. I got letters from Mr. Pelham in Nairobi, and all this food, and with money inside-- very nice, very good. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I wrote him letters. I found a letter of mine, written in French, talking about the future, that we have got to put our-- our goodwill-- I can't even tell you. The letter-- I read it through the other day. I had tears in my eyes. Did I write this letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: And anyhow, I knew that Jack would not be coming to Beira. I knew he was coming to Umtali, which was the frontier between-- between Rhodesia-- southern Rhodesia-- and Mozambique. So I knew he wasn't coming. He had told me that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there would be a Mr. Schwartz who would come onto the boat and fetch me. And he would-- he would give me money so I can buy an engagement ring. Engagement ring? I don't even know the guy. I know only the letters. I wasn't going to buy an engagement ring.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, everybody-- yes, as the tide was out, everybody got off the boat and I was left. Another one or two were left. And I was left. And I said, OK, when the tide comes in, I will go into-- we will get into harbor, and Mr. Schwartz will come and fetch me. There was a call. I don't-- I-- it must have been an intercom, or I was called. I don't know. Ms. Erder is wanted in the library. So I went down, and I see a short man standing with his back to me. I said, no, he has come. He has come!

speaker: interviewee
text: And the heart starts beating. I was 21 at that time. Just after my birthday you see? And-- and I said, yes? Not in English. And the man turns around, and it is not Jack. But he was short. Good looking young man, blue, blue eyes.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my-- my name is Hoffman. Willie Hoffman, I think he was. No, not [INAUDIBLE] Hoffman. Hoffman. His name was Hoffman. Forgot his first name. I came to fetch you from the boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, is that so? Where's Mr. Schwartz? I was cheeky. So he says, it's Mr. Schwartz that sent me. I said, I'm sorry. I will not go with-- you see, the thing is, I will tell you why I did that.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was very-- was terrified of white trade. White-- white trade means--

speaker: interviewer
text: Slave--

speaker: interviewee
text: What you call them? Slave trade, white slave trade. To-- they take them off the boat, the girls, and take them to a beautiful place, and then they make them prostitutes, you see? My parents were terrified, and they had written a letter to London, to the-- it's for the rabbi did that, you see? To the society in London. London had written a-- a letter to the rabbi in Salisbury, whose name was Rabbi Konwiza. You had him here for a while. And he had written to another-- another couple, a Mr. and Mrs. Barnett, who were big wigs in-- in Beira. Very big, like governors, very big.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the-- the Barnetts had received the letter. Oh, what an honor. We're going to fetch this Ms. Erder, you see? So they heard that there was a Mr. Schwartz to fetch her. They said, don't fetch her. We will see to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But Mr. Schwartz said, but my wife already prepared the lokshen soup and [INAUDIBLE] soup and all this. And he said, don't worry. My wife also has got. So that were-- was why Mr. Schwartz didn't come to the boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, Mr. Schwartz won't come to the boat, said Mr. Hoffman, because it has been-- now Mr. Schwartz had given it over to Mr. Barnett. I said, who is Mr. Barnett? He says, that's my boss. And he-- and he sent me. Oh, I thought to myself. He says, look, no, but Mr. Schwartz won't come here. So I thought to myself, what can happen to me? If he wants to take me somewhere, we'll just not go. I'll scream like anything. So I went into a boat, little boat, and we were being row-- rowed to the-- to the cay. And then in the boat, he tells me, I believe you're going to Salisbury to marry a man. I said, yes. He says, don't go to Salisbury. Marry me.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a refugee, a German refugee. Don't go to Salisbury. Marry me. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said-- I looked at him. I couldn't believe my eyes-- my ears. And he said-- and I said to him, I gave my promise to marry Mr. Pelham on the 25th of June, and marry him I will. And poor guy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later-- later on, he came to Salisbury. And we didn't really befriend each other, but we knew each other. And the poor guy never married. I was so sorry for him, really. He was a nice man, and he was a victim of Nazism, another victim of Nazism. And there were plenty in the-- not-- not the Holocaust only. These were victims because he-- he went to drink eventually because he had no future.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we arrived. And I said, Mr. Hoffman-- and then I spoke German to him because he-- I could hear the accent. I want-- I want-- the first thing I want you to do is take me to the post office. He says, certainly. There was a car waiting. And he drives, and didn't take long. How big is Beira?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING PORTUGUESE] Yeah. All right. I went in there, sent a telegram to my parents. 'Arrived safe and sound, all well.' I wanted my parents to be quiet. There was-- I was a month on the boat. And for that, my mom-- later I heard that my mother had taken so ill that she couldn't even get out of-- of bed. First of all, she was frail. Secondly, she-- her wonderful precious child, her-- the apple of her eye had gone, knowing she would never see me again. And that-- it was devastating. She was very ill, apparently. That, I got from my surviving brother. I have one surviving brother. That my father had to carry her out and take her to the-- to the-- there was a park nearby. And there, she sort of slowly, slowly came to herself. So at least-- I didn't know that at that-- at that time, of course. They had a telegram to say, arrived safe, all fine. Now I said, you take me to Mr. Schwartz. And sure enough, he takes-- drives, and there's a little Schwartz and Son. I said, fine, you wait here. And I go inside. Mr. Schwartz, I am Santa Erder, and you were supposed to fetch me, and I was boat-- waiting on the boat. And he looked at me and he smiled. He says, Fraulein Erder-- or-- you know. I said, I want to tell you something. I-- I was coming. And then that happened, and I cannot fight this family. You go. You are in good hands. Don't worry. They want [INAUDIBLE]. Go, you'll be fine. But take this. So I open it up, and there was a check for a diamond ring. And I thought to myself, I could easily take for this money-- it-- I don't know how-- 200 pounds or whatever it was. I don't remember. It was money, quite a bit of money. He couldn't afford it, believe me, at that time. But he wanted to welcome me. That was really wonderful of him. And he is a wonderful man, you know. Renia didn't lie. And I could easily have now a nice ring for this, you see. I put the envelope with the check. I said, thank you very much, put it into my bag. And off I went. And I come now to-- first he took me to Mr. Barnett. Mr. Barnett took one look at me and he approved. And then he took me to Mrs. Barnett. And as I walked in there-- a mansion. Because he was-- he wasn't the governor, but he was like the one who-- he was Jewish. And he came from Johannesburg. But he had worked himself up, and he was the figurehead there. Anything that happened, it came to him. And there was a roomful of people. It was June. It was holiday time. It was, of course, beautiful weather in Beira. And they were all ex-- they were all Rhodesians. Not ex. Rhodesians on holiday. And she entertained the lot. And there were young Spanish people. They started speaking Spanish with me. It was a wonderful reception. What a lovely thing I had here. And eventually, the-- the-- the luncheon was over. And it wasn't a luncheon. It was a morning. And they went. And she gave her time to me, and then her husband arrived for lunch. And we're sitting there. Yes. On-- in the letter, my husband said to me, when you come, do not divulge that we don't know each other. We don't know what can happen to us when we are married. We hope for the best, but say that we met once or twice. Because Jack had actually been-- during the time that I was there-- before Puterflam got-- met my father, Jack had actually been look-- been with-- with-- with the Puterflam family for a few weeks while he was in France. But he had gone back and had come to Rhodesia at that time. So it was like 18 months before this happened with me. And so the letter said not to divulge that we didn't know each other. That we had met. We had seen each other. So that stuck, actually, in my mind. I mustn't say that we didn't know each other. And then-- and then--

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES] How did you get to meet Jack eventually?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Now, then Mrs.-- now, then, Mrs.-- Mrs. Barnett said something. You know who she's going to marry? She's going to marry Mr. Pelham. He said, oh, who is this Mr. Pelham?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she says, you met him at cards. You met him-- you know, the shorty like that. I said, short like that? I gave away the-- The secret.

speaker: interviewee
text: The what?

speaker: interviewer
text: The secret.

speaker: interviewee
text: The secret that I didn't know him. Oh, she said, I thought you knew him. Oh, I said, yes, but not short like that. Anyhow, I got-- now they take me to the train. And the whole boat who knew me, the Jewish people, came to the boat to wish me well. And so it was that on-- I-- I-- Oh, yes. The ticket was booked for first class. I had a whole first class ticket to myself. And the train pulled out. And as it pulled out, it stopped at smaller station. And at the small station, there were these little pickaninnies, the little black men, selling things, you know, whatever they make out of ivory or so. This was black. And I saw a little pickaninny with two candlesticks. And my father's words stuck into my mind-- in my mind. They were about that high. They made out of wood, and they were black. You know, beautifully shaped with a-- with a base.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I look at these candlesticks. And I don't-- what language, I don't remember. I spoke to him, and I-- I pointed. And he told me something, and I took out five pounds. I give him five-- you know what five pounds? It's riches for them! Five pounds was a hell of a lot of money. But Jack had sent me money, you see? And I gave it to him. He gave me the candlesticks. He ran! He was afraid!

speaker: interviewee
text: I had my candlesticks. I didn't know if Jack was religious, not religious, ex-religious. As a matter of fact, he did have candlesticks, which he lit himself before the marriage because he came also from a very religious home. And so it was that I went on. Now, in-- all of the sudden, I hear the-- the train jarring. The train jarred and jarred. And it woke me up. And it was-- it was midnight. Sort.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
